passages.text:
... new and sudden and unexpected which appears before
us, creates a movement in our soul, almost contradictory in
itself of believing and not believing. Of believing because the
thing is seen to exist; and of not believing because it is sudden,
new, and not before either known or dreamt of.”12 The
meraviglia, the characteristic end of all poetry, is found at the
intersection of two orders of things, those that are possible and
those that are impossible, fantastic and irrational. From the
combination of these two orders whatever is possible may
acquire the charming and mysterious face of the...

<!-- Page 3 -->

appearance of verisimilitude and coherence.14 The dialectic
tension of such a combination of opposites provokes the
beholder’s astonishment and marvel.
Some of the most marvelous examples of Patrizi’s poetics are
indeed to be found at Bomarzo and, as Bélanger has pointed
out, meraviglia may have been used by Orsini to present his
guests with his personal view of a world in which the truth
resides in doubts and the suspension of all judgment.15 Let us
take for example the leaning house, (figure 1), which represents
the abode of a wealthy Roman. An inscription seems to...

... adesso guardare la Primavera con gli oc-
chi del Magnifico.
Alla sua destra la bellezza terrestre, mortale e
ingannevole della defunta Simonetta:
Morí, come disopra dicemo, nella città nostra una don-
na, la quale se mosse a compassione generalmente tut-
to el populo fiorentino, non è gran meraviglia, perché
di bellezza e gentilezza umana era veramente ornata
quanto alcuna che innanzi a lei fussi suta. E fra l’altre
sue eccellenti dote aveva cosí dolce e attrattiva maniera,
che tutti quelli che con lei avevono qualche domestica
notizia credevono da essa sommamente essere amati.1
Le...

... impiamente trahere senza alcuna dementia, che 
unquantulo in essi non era proma.1 Più spavento et terrore mi misseron 
che alla casta Lucretia Sexto Tarquinio cum la evaginata spatha in 
mano la opprobriosa morte minitante. Là onde senza spirito, oltra il 
credere isbigotita, ad un’hora meraviglia et timore me incusseno gli diri 
et sanguinarii homini, in tanto che, evacuata et exinanita, ciascuna vena 
al doloroso et moerente core concorse, più timida effecta d’una dammula 
et più pavida che il aurito et timoroso lepore2 tra gli densi arbusculi et 
ioncosi cespiti latitante ode...

... of wonderment that is at work in the
Hypnerotomachia is indebted to the cultural milieu in which the
book was conceived. To a certain extent, the monastic prac-
tices of meditation, with which a Dominican friar like Colonna
must have been familiar,36 bear many affinities to Patrizi’s
poetics of meraviglia. This would explain why the backward
model of Patrizi’s work is partly but not entirely plausible.
As Mary Carruthers has shown in her study of medieval
rhetoric, meditation practice in monastic circles incorporated
fundamentally an art of memory, which included “the essential
roles of...

... in Renaissance
Poetics,” Review of English Studies 43, no. 171 (August 1992): 387–94.
12 – Patrizi, Della poetica, vol. 2, 365.
13 – Danilo Aguzzi Barbagli, “Introduzione,” in Francesco Patrizi, Della
poetica, vol. 1, xv.
14 – Anne Bélanger, Bomarzo ou les incertitudes de la lecture: Figure de la
meraviglia dans un jardin maniériste du XVI siècle (Paris: Honoré Champion,
2007), 250.
15 – Ibid., 289.
16 – The Latin inscription reads: Animus quiescendo fit prudentior ergo.
17 – Bélanger, Bomarzo ou les incertitudes de la lecture, 251.
